question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers: nan

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers:
To provide an up-to-date picture of the clinical effi-cacy and safety of IL-17 antagonists in psoriasis, we conducted a meta-analysis

Studies included in this meta-analysis met the follow criteria: (i) they were phase 3 RCTs that enrolled patients with plaque psoriasis; (ii) their duration of treatment was >=12 weeks; (iii) they used a parallel design or crossover design of IL-17 antagonists vs. placebo; and (iv) their data reported information on Psoriasis Area and Severity Index (PASI) response, Dermatology Life Quality Index (DLQI) score of 0 or 1 response, adverse events (AEs) and discontinuations.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in selection of studies?
answers:
Study selection was performed by two independent investigators.
First, titles and abstracts of cited articles were evaluated by
two investigators to determine their relevance. Then, full papers
were reviewed to identify all trials meeting the eligibility criteria.
Disagreements were resolved through discussion or by consensus
with a third investigator.

answers: nan

question: Was risk of bias (or methodological quality) formally assessed using appropriate criteria?
answers: Methodological quality of included articles was assessed using modified Jadad criteria with an eight-item scale (randomization, method of randomization, blinding, method of blinding, withdrawals and dropouts, inclusion and exclusion criteria, adverse effects and statistical analysis) by two independent reviewers. The majority of studies retained were allocated high-quality scores (mean modified Jadad score 7.875 for included studies, all of the nine RCTs had a score >=7).

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in risk of bias assessment?
answers: nan

question: Did the synthesis include all studies that it should?
answers: A total of 405 relevant articles were identified from various databases, of which 359 articles were excluded after reviewing the titles and abstracts. Then, an additional 37 articles were excluded due to various reasons (not RCTs, not phase 3 trials or no required data). Finally, nine articles27-35 including nine RCTs fully met the eligibility criteria. Nine RCTs with 5941 patients reported the proportion of patients with a 75% improvement from baseline in the PASI score (PASI 75) at 12 weeks. Nine RCTs with 5941 patients reported PASI 90 response rates. Nine RCTs with 5941 patients reported the PASI 100 response rates at 12 weeks.

question: Were all pre-defined analyses reported or departures explained?
answers: nan

question: Was the synthesis appropriate given the nature and similarity in the research questions, study designs and outcomes across included studies?
answers: The statistical analysis was performed using Review Manager 5.2 (The Nordic Cochrane Centre, Copenhagen, Denmark) from the Cochrane Collaboration. All analysis indicators, which were categorical dichotomous variables, were assessed using relative risks (RR). P < 0.05 was considered to be statistically significant, and 95% confidence intervals (CI) were reported. Homogeneity was tested by the I2 statistic (significance level at I2 > 50%). 26 A random-effects model was used if the I2 statistic was significant. Otherwise, a fixed-effects model was used.

question: Was between-study variation (heterogeneity) minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers:
The IL-17 antagonists group was associated with a higher rate of DLQI 0 or 1 response than the placebo group (RR = 7.11, 95% CI: 4.18-12.09, P < 0.00001) with significant heterogeneity between studies (P = 0.0005, I2 = 80%; Fig. 5)

s. IL-17 antagonists were associated with a higher infection proportion than the placebo group (RR = 1.29, 95% CI: 1.06-1.57, P = 0.01) with significant heterogeneity (P = 0.02,I2 = 56%; Fig. 8).

question: Were the findings robust, e.g. as demonstrated through funnel plot or sensitivity analyses?
answers: Our results are mainly based on individual case reports, smallcase  series  and  retrospective  studies.  Treatment options include topical, physical, surgery, systemic  treatments andphototherapy. Treatments were often reported to have beenunsuccessful. The disease course after treatment tends to beprogressive with occurrence of new lesions. Medical literature also shows that treatment of monoclonal gammopathy withalkylating agents does not necessarily influence the activity of skin disease.

question: Were biases in primary studies minimal or addressed in the synthesis?

The majority of studies retained were allocated high-quality scores (mean modified Jadad score 7.875 for included studies, all of the nine RCTs had a score >=7).
